title: What's Driving Rising Business Costs?

source.name: New York Fed Liberty Street Economics

summary: The New York Fed's regional business surveys show that business cost increases accelerated significantly in 2025, with manufacturers facing 8.5% cost increases and service firms facing 7% increases, up from approximately 5% in 2024. Employee health insurance costs saw the largest increases at 12.9-14.2%, followed by utilities at 8.5% for both firm types, while tariffs contributed to goods and materials cost increases for manufacturers. The survey data indicates cost pressures are expected to moderate in 2026 to 4.8-5.4%.
key_points: Manufacturing firms reported total cost increases of approximately 8.5% in 2025, up from 5% in 2024, while service firms reported 7% increases, up from 5% in 2024
Employee health insurance costs rose 14.2% for manufacturers and 12.9% for service firms, with some firms reporting increases of 25-50% at renewal
Utilities costs increased 8.5% for both firm types, with about 15% of respondents reporting increases of 20% or more, partly tied to AI-related data center growth
Business insurance costs rose approximately 7-7.5% for service and manufacturing firms respectively, with roughly 1 in 10 firms reporting increases exceeding 20%
Tariffs contributed to goods and materials cost increases, particularly affecting aluminum, steel, equipment, electrical supplies, auto parts, coffee, and cocoa inputs
Cost increases are projected to moderate in 2026 to 5.4% for service firms and 4.8% for manufacturers
why_it_matters: Rising business costs directly affect corporate profit margins and pricing power, serving as a key input for inflation expectations. The sharp acceleration in 2025 beyond expectations suggests potential for sustained margin compression or pass-through to customers, which could influence Federal Reserve policy decisions and business investment sentiment. The differential impact on manufacturers versus service firms also has implications for sector-specific competitiveness.
macro_context: The data indicates cost-push inflationary pressures remain significant despite expectations for moderation. The acceleration in 2025 beyond forecasts suggests inflation dynamics may be stickier than anticipated. Rising business costs can feed into final goods prices, maintaining inflationary risks even as growth moderates.
market_structure_context: Regional business survey data from the New York-Northern New Jersey area shows structural differences in cost composition between manufacturers and service firms, with manufacturers facing larger utilities and materials cost burdens due to greater tariff exposure. The survey uses trimmed mean methodology excluding the highest and lowest 5% of responses, which provides robust central tendency estimates but may understate extreme experiences.

issues_found: Future publication date (March 2026) referencing 2025 data is unusual but source authenticity appears valid; New York Fed Liberty Street Economics is a credible source
Event type 'cost_dynamics_report' is appropriate for this content

Topic classification under 'market structure' is somewhat tangential; this is primarily a regional economic conditions/macro report with secondary market structure relevance through sector cost differentials

human_review.notes: Source is a credible Federal Reserve Bank publication with strong factual support for all major claims. All numerical values in key_points are directly traceable to source data. Regional scope limitations are properly acknowledged. The unusual publication date referencing past-year data appears to be standard Fed blog practice. No unsupported claims identified.

notes: Source is from a credible central bank research blog. Regional scope (New York-Northern New Jersey) limits national generalizability. Uses trimmed mean methodology. Published date of March 2026 appears to be a future date relative to content discussing 2025 data. The date may be an error in source metadata, but the content should be treated as historical data point. Third post in a three-part series; other posts cover health insurance impacts on wages and pricing behavior.
